name: Turkish Folk Magic
description: Turkish Folk Magic, deeply intertwined with Anatolian history and Islamic influences, encompasses a range of practices aimed at influencing fortune, health, and relationships. Rooted in pre-Islamic Turkic shamanism and interwoven with Islamic beliefs, it utilizes amulets, spells, rituals, and folk remedies. Practices vary regionally, often involving the recitation of prayers, verses from the Quran, and the use of symbolic objects. Amulets (muska) are frequently worn for protection against the evil eye (nazar) and other malevolent forces. Dream interpretation (rüya tabiri) holds significance, offering insights into the future or hidden aspects of life. Healing practices involve herbal remedies, incantations, and rituals performed by healers. This magic operates within a framework of belief in spirits (cinler) and the power of intention (niyet). Its cultural significance lies in providing solace, hope, and a sense of control in a world perceived as influenced by supernatural forces. It serves as a means of coping with misfortune, seeking blessings, and maintaining social harmony. The oral tradition plays a crucial role in transmitting knowledge and practices across generations. While viewed skeptically by some, it remains a significant aspect of Turkish cultural heritage, particularly in rural areas.
subtitle: A blend of tradition and faith.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of the Evil Eye
keyMyths.description: The Evil Eye (Nazar) is believed to be a curse transmitted through a malicious glare, causing misfortune. Amulets, often blue and shaped like an eye, are worn to deflect this negative energy.

keyMyths.title: The Story of Hızır
keyMyths.description: Hızır is a revered figure in Turkish folklore, often associated with helping those in need and bestowing blessings. He is believed to appear to people in disguise, offering guidance and assistance.
